title: Sled Runners
desc: The transport sleds that move crystal through the narrow lanes are dragging again, which means more friction than anyone wants near fragile cargo.

steps.text:
Crystal Cave does not permit ordinary carts for most of its interior work. The lanes are too narrow, the turns too sharp, and the crystal edges too unforgiving. Instead, crews use low transport sleds fitted with felt-lined runners that slide over the smoothed work tracks. When the runners are in good order, the load glides. When they are not, the sled chatters, catches, and reminds everyone how expensive a broken crystal spine can be.

The track keeper kneels beside a stalled sled with a fistful of felt pads and a brush. "Replace the runner lining if you want to work on the sled," she says. "Or clear the track grooves ahead if you prefer to deal with the cave rather than the equipment. One fixes the pull. The other fixes what it pulls over."

options.label: Replace the worn felt on the sled runners
Clear the track grooves and loose shards ahead

steps.text:
The runner pads are thin, polished smooth by too many trips and one bad habit of moving loaded sleds farther than the maintenance marks recommend. You pry the dead felt free, fit fresh strips into the grooves, and trim them flush so nothing bunches when the weight comes on. The keeper tests the first drag by hand. This time the sled moves with a quiet slide instead of the irritated rasp it had before.

Once the whole lining is renewed, even the empty frame feels lighter. The keeper seems almost cheerful about it, which in Crystal Cave counts as emotional excess. "Good," she says. "Now the sled can remember it was built to glide, not to argue."

options.label: Stack the old felt and clear the sled for loading

steps.text:
The track grooves collect exactly what you would expect in a cave full of sharp stone: splinters of crystal, fine red grit, and the occasional loose shard waiting for a runner to find it the unpleasant way. You brush and scrape the grooves section by section, clearing the bends first where friction multiplies and patience usually dies. By the time you finish, the lane reads like a route again instead of a slow mechanical threat.

The keeper runs the empty sled over the cleaned track and the difference carries in the sound. No chatter. No little catches at the turns. Just a steady whisper against stone. She glances down the lane, then back at you. "There. Better. The cave can keep its edges. We only ask that it stop hiding them in the rails."

options.label: Brush the last red grit aside and reopen the lane
